skill.name: pixi-package-manager
skill.description: Guides pixi package manager workflows for reproducible scientific Python environments using conda-forge and PyPI dependencies.
skill.summary: Build reproducible scientific Python projects with pixi, conda-forge, PyPI, environments, tasks, and CI.
skill.author: CodingKaiser

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but review shows the skill is mostly pixi documentation with expected package-manager examples. The remaining real concerns are security-sensitive guidance such as curl piped to bash and eval-based shell activation, so publication is reasonable with warnings rather than a block.

medium_findings.title: Package Manager Commands Can Modify Local Environments
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many pixi, uv, conda, and shell command examples that install packages, update lockfiles, run tasks, or publish artifacts. These are expected for a pixi package-manager skill, but an assistant should only execute them with user intent and after reviewing project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are real and numerous, but they are documentation for the advertised package-management function. I found no evidence that the skill runs commands automatically or hides malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: A GitLab CI example installs pixi with a curl-to-bash pipeline. This is a known risky pattern because the downloaded script executes immediately in CI, although the URL points to the pixi project installer and the context is documentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in a CI setup example. The risk is real, but the surrounding context is legitimate installation guidance rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Shell Hook Evaluation Example
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting section recommends eval on pixi shell-hook output inside a bash script. This is a legitimate environment activation pattern, but eval executes generated shell code and should not be used with untrusted pixi binaries or project state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval command substitution is explicit and security-sensitive. The command source is pixi itself, so the risk depends on tool and project trust rather than a malicious payload in the skill.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are official pixi, GitHub, conda-forge, PyPI, and scientific Python resource links. I found no evidence that the skill sends data to these endpoints or uses them for exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible documentation resources and ecosystem references. No network client code or secret-bearing request is present in the inspected content.

low_findings.title: Relative Editable Package Paths Are Normal Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections occur in editable local dependency examples that refer to sibling packages. They are not file-read or file-write operations and do not attempt to escape a sandbox.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../ paths appear inside TOML examples for local package development. This is a common package-manager feature, not an exploit path by itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are prose, project metadata, package names, or documentation text rather than MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic operations. No cryptographic API use was found in the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Inspection of the cited lines showed plain markdown content and examples, not cryptographic implementation. The scanner appears to have matched incidental words.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Redirection Detections Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden path and device-file detections are normal shell examples: adding $HOME/.pixi/bin to PATH and redirecting stderr while trying package sources. They do not access sensitive files or devices.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited filesystem-like patterns are visible installation and shell-redirection examples. They do not show credential access, destructive file operations, or hidden persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: A GitLab CI example installs pixi with a curl-to-bash pipeline. This is a known risky pattern because the downloaded script executes immediately in CI, although the URL points to the pixi project installer and the context is documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in a CI setup example. The risk is real, but the surrounding context is legitimate installation guidance rather than confirmed malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Hook Evaluation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting section recommends eval on pixi shell-hook output inside a bash script. This is a legitimate environment activation pattern, but eval executes generated shell code and should not be used with untrusted pixi binaries or project state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The eval command substitution is explicit and security-sensitive. The command source is pixi itself, so the risk depends on tool and project trust rather than a malicious payload in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Commands Can Modify Local Environments
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many pixi, uv, conda, and shell command examples that install packages, update lockfiles, run tasks, or publish artifacts. These are expected for a pixi package-manager skill, but an assistant should only execute them with user intent and after reviewing project files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are real and numerous, but they are documentation for the advertised package-management function. I found no evidence that the skill runs commands automatically or hides malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Scientific Python projects often mix compiled packages, PyPI tools, and reproducibility needs. This skill guides pixi setup, dependency strategy, environments, tasks, CI, and migration workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use pixi for scientific Python projects with compiled dependencies.
Shows how to initialize pixi projects with pyproject.toml and import existing environments.
Guides dependency placement across conda-forge packages, PyPI dependencies, and feature environments.
Provides patterns for tasks, testing, documentation builds, and CI workflows.
Covers migration from requirements.txt, environment.yml, conda, mamba, and uv workflows.
limitations: Does not verify package safety or vulnerability status automatically.
Assumes pixi or a compatible installation method is available before commands run.
Examples may need adjustment for private package indexes, corporate proxies, or air-gapped systems.
Does not replace human review of dependency updates, lockfile changes, or publish commands.

use_cases.title: Start a Reproducible Research Project
use_cases.description: Create a pixi project that pins Python, scientific packages, and tasks for repeatable analysis across machines.

use_cases.title: Modernize Legacy Conda Workflows
use_cases.description: Convert environment.yml or requirements.txt workflows into pyproject.toml with pixi environments and lockfiles.

use_cases.title: Standardize Scientific Package Development
use_cases.description: Organize runtime, development, test, documentation, and release tasks for a scientific Python package.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Pixi Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new scientific Python project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a basic pixi setup for a new scientific Python project using NumPy, Pandas, pytest, and ruff.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate Existing Dependencies
prompt_templates.scenario: Move from requirements.txt or environment.yml to pixi.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my existing dependency files and propose a pixi migration plan with conda-forge and PyPI package placement.

prompt_templates.title: Design Multi-Environment Workflows
prompt_templates.scenario: Separate development, testing, documentation, and GPU environments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a pixi environment strategy for dev, test, docs, CPU, and GPU workflows in a scientific Python repository.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Pixi CI Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of reproducibility and CI safety.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pixi CI workflow for reproducibility, caching, lockfile use, package source safety, and risky shell commands.

output_examples.input: I need a reproducible environment for climate data analysis.
output_examples.output: A concise pixi setup plan with Python version guidance, conda-forge package choices, PyPI-only package notes, and repeatable analysis tasks.

output_examples.input: My project has requirements.txt and environment.yml files.
output_examples.output: A migration checklist that separates compiled scientific packages, pure Python packages, development tools, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: I want CI to run tests and documentation builds with pixi.
output_examples.output: A workflow review that covers pixi setup, dependency installation, task naming, caching, and safer handling of installer commands.

best_practices: Review lockfile changes before merging dependency updates.
Prefer conda-forge for compiled scientific packages and PyPI for packages unavailable there.
Confirm user intent before running install, update, publish, or shell activation commands.
anti_patterns: Running remote installer scripts in CI without reviewing or pinning the installer source.
Mixing conda and PyPI dependencies without documenting why each source was chosen.
Treating generated dependency migrations as final without testing imports and workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan pixi projects, dependencies, environments, tasks, CI workflows, and migration from older Python environment tools.

faq.question: Does it install pixi automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Users should approve installation commands before execution.

faq.question: Can it replace conda or mamba workflows?
faq.answer: It can guide migration to pixi when a project benefits from conda-forge packages, PyPI integration, and lockfiles.

faq.question: Is it only for Python projects?
faq.answer: It focuses on scientific Python, but pixi can also manage dependencies from ecosystems such as R, Julia, C, and C++.

faq.question: How should teams use the generated commands?
faq.answer: Teams should review commands, package sources, and lockfile changes before running them in shared repositories or CI.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate documentation, but it includes shell commands, package installs, network installer guidance, and eval-based activation examples.
